items.q: Does a star's right ascension change through the night like clock time?
items.correct: No - it stays essentially fixed; RA and declination are nearly constant for a star.
items.misconception: Yes, RA changes every hour like a clock.
items.random_wrong: RA depends on where you stand on Earth.
items.why: RA and dec are equatorial coordinates fixed to the sky, unlike altitude and azimuth which vary.

items.q: What does declination correspond to on Earth?
items.correct: Latitude - degrees north or south of the celestial equator.
items.why: Declination mirrors latitude on the celestial sphere, while right ascension mirrors longitude.

items.q: Why is the ecliptic tilted to the celestial equator?
items.correct: Because Earth's axis is tilted about 23.5 degrees.
items.misconception: Because the Sun orbits at an angle by chance.
items.random_wrong: Because of the Moon's gravitational pull.
items.why: The axial tilt that gives us seasons also tilts the Sun's apparent path away from the equator.

items.q: Right ascension is the celestial equivalent of:
items.why: It's Longitude. “Latitude” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Declination is measured in degrees north or south of the:
items.why: Correct answer: Celestial equator. “Horizon” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The ecliptic is tilted to the celestial equator by about:
items.why: It's 23.5 degrees. “0 degrees” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Local sidereal time equals the right ascension of an object that is:
items.why: It's On the observer's meridian. “On the horizon” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: By how many degrees is the ecliptic tilted to the celestial equator?
items.why: The real figure is 23.5 degrees. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
